dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 en route for a traffic/transportation incident, report back later.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911?
I have lower back pain and numbness in my legs from a car accident.
Can you tell me your name for us, please?
I'm Sarah, S-A-R-A-H.
Good. What city are we in right now?
We're in [CITY], I think.
Do you know today's date or what day it is?
She is oriented to person, place, and time appropriately.
What happened just before the accident, if you can recall?
I was turning onto a ramp, lost control, then hit another car there.
Ma'am, can you tell us any long-term medical conditions or allergies you have?
I have type 1 diabetes, hypertension, and bipolar disorder.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how severe is the back pain you're feeling right now?
It's a 10-very persistent and getting worse with movements.
Is the pain sharp, dull, throbbing, or something else in your back or legs?
It's aching in my back and numbing in my legs, especially on the right side.
When did the pain start after the accident, if you can say?
She said the pain began the moment she felt the impact of the car crash.
We need to check your airway to ensure it is clear. Is that okay with you, Sarah?
Yes, go ahead.
I don't see any bruising, burns, or rashes on her skin surface at this time.
Pulse is strong and regular bilaterally. No signs of poor perfusion detected.
Blood pressure is elevated at 209 over 73 mmHg, as recorded initially.
Pulse is 86, and the rhythm is regular.
Respirations are 16, per minute, and unlabored with good chest rise and fall.
Oxygen saturation is 90% on room air as of the first measurement.
Glucose reading is 0, not able to establish more data at this time.
EKG tracing shows a normal sinus rhythm without any dysrhythmia at this time.
Can you be more specific about the numbness and the weakness side, please?
It's both legs, but the right one is worse. Pain keeps me from moving them much.
Will you try lifting your arms for us as part of our neuro assessment now?
I can lift them when not in pain.
Can you attempt to move or feel your legs for us now? That will help us assess function.
I can't move them too much because of the pain. I just feel numbness and weakness there.
We are going to place an IV in your left hand if you're okay with that for fluids and meds, if needed.
IV access placed in the left hand after several attempts, secured with gauze and tape there.
Rechecked blood glucose levels but still read 0 per current clinical testing methods.
We'll now apply a 4-lead ECG for continuous monitoring to track your heart rhythm in transit.
EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm now with no changes from baseline recording obtained earlier.
We are going to follow protocol 4-1 for general trauma management on this patient's condition.
We are ready to transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] as the closest appropriate facility nearby for care needed.
I understand. Please get me to the hospital as soon as you can for professional evaluation here.
We will now transfer you onto the stretcher via a backboard to ensure safety for transport there.
Transfer is complete. You are now secured for loading and transporting safely to the facility.
Let me confirm your vital signs again before we start transport for baseline tracking during the trip there.
Your pulse is now at 96 beats per minute and regular with no tachycardia or dysrhythmia present now.
Your blood pressure is 208 over 78-continued hypertension, and we will watch for any changes in transit to the hospital.
Breathing remains at 16, unlabored, and no signs of respiratory distress on recheck as per previous baseline.
Oxygen saturation has dropped to 0 at this point with none observed in repeated readings for clarification here.
Just to check again, how is the severity of the back pain now since we reassess on the way to the facility here now?
It is still a 10, no changes noted with movement restrictions maintained and reported here per patient self-assessment.
We are en route to the hospital now and will continue monitoring your status for any emergent developments during travel to acute care facility there.
Vital signs remain within stable limits at this time as we begin transport with no immediate alterations observed per current readings here in real-time.
She refused the cervical collar when asked, but we confirmed no signs of cervical trauma upon primary or secondary exams performed here on scene earlier on arrival at the incident site to her.

We have arrived at the hospital. She is stable, and no life-threatening conditions were identified here in field-based examination before your arrival of unit at trauma center for further assessment here now per protocol.

IV access established on scene, and no pain medications were administered in the field during transport due to access limitations caused by medical conditions in situ here during incident review with patient before arrival at scheduled trauma facility for clinical review here now by nursing team at emergency department waiting during implementation of standard high-acuity protocols for back injury and leg trauma involved in collision prior to 911 contact using current medical history for prioritization of level of care for acute episode of trauma requiring continuous observation post-delivery to medical team for progression and resolution of care and recovery needs post-assessment by hospital staff further once there to evaluate her complete status for inclusion into emergency team protocols to assign trauma resources here on site once safely discharged from ambulance for transition onto critical care onto facility equipment already pending any emergent life-threatening complications requiring immediate surgical or medical intervention to preserve neurological stability here per ongoing monitoring and communication during transport to destination medical center assigned in the field by EMT team before arriving here on CareLink drop communication currently transmitting data to hospital's prior alert system to prepare team in advance for unknown potential injury severity prior to knowing her full condition until on scene. All received information is current while on route. Checks completed and confirmed stable condition for handoff. No signs of hypoperfusion, shock, or respiratory distress, as of last check before arrival for smooth and safe onboard integration into facility for emergency care. All equipment and data provided to emergency reception team here for continuity of care. No nasal cannula or high-flow oxygen provided in transit due to 0 saturation readings after attempted access to system. Provider has initiated a standard IV to ensure access to fluids during hospital-based assessments to provide stability during initial treatment for patient here during next diagnostic phase. No prior adverse events occurred in course of transport except for equipment and IV setup while on move. Emergency department care has been initiated and confirmed. All logs forwarded and EMT staff stays for matters related to patient handover for further reassurance of treatment at clinical level for next phase protocols. Patient reported light and alertness at last physical status observation in ambulance with continuous back pain, no more than that. All systems show control priors for no intervention in pre-arrival procedure except for IV setup for readiness in case prior compatibility or supply access is required into forced intervention here due to unknowns at time of arrival. All prior consent for transport and procedures have been collected from patient as of initiation of EMT services here in field. No family on scene at time of incident. No bystander intervention required. Per patient estimation and admission, no injuries are suspected to head or neck, but symptomatology in legs are a concern, hence protocol 4-1 is being followed with evidence-based data exchanged in advance. Last check showed unchanged vitals during motion as well but stable with low abnormalities for non-life-threatening concerns. No alerts issued at time of handover. All systems in check for medical care transfer. No complications during transit to destination per documented responses. No tools missing. Caravan is ready for further transfers if required. Patient can now be safely handed off to the ED team. All clinical data provided for continuity of care. EMT team will stay until patient is handed over and transported safely onto facility for further evaluation. No further movement requested unless abnormalities detected mid-handover. Equipment and patient integrity confirmed for optimal delivery of care. No injuries found during re-assessments following primary and secondary assessments before start of transport._condition.

Partner transferring patient to hospital as guided per previous field assessments for next phase of acute care abroad with no anticipated medical complications during and after transition. No reversal of admitted symptomatology or forced withdrawal required from patient prior to delivery onto hospital for continuity of treatment. No unexplainable erratic behaviors during treatment here as of now. No complications noted in IV access per protocol in use for treatment. IV functioning as of last monitored access placement and confirmed as satisfactory for future potential use while hooked into monitoring through IV function. No further interventions were given during transport due to non-complexity of patient's condition during en route monitoring and movement to hospital. Utilized standard transport methods for trauma cases with appropriate padding and positioning for spinal precautions as per patient's own statement of no cervical pain or impaired website access to neurological symptoms during own reporting before all en route procedures commenced. Standard blood pressure support and monitoring performed at regular intervals during ride to facility for readiness of emergency care. All pre-transport patient evaluation protocols followed and recorded properly for inclusion into facility's electronic health record system for further care, documentation and tracking to provide accuracy in medical research and audits post-discharge. Prior field observations are consistent with those taken during arrival and transit. No additional or new issues came up during movement. Patient was cooperative during extrication and transfer to ambulance. No history of refusal to care or delays in treatment even before extricating her from scene. Management of back injury and leg infirmity was the focus of initial care and transfer. No need to inform for further complications or alert for any of potential chronic conditions during this phased treatment process. Patient denies other symptoms of concern post-incident. No signs of nausea, vomiting, dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath, or abdominal pain in any time of assessment or transport to facility. All concerns seem to stem from trauma, as evidenced in patient-reported incident and EMT confirmed evaluation. The IV and ECG are functioning as intended for standard trauma patient care protocols. No adverse events during transport to hospital facility. No resuscitation needs for patient as of this handoff here. Preparatory measures for hospital integration are completed and verified in real-time for accurate institutional acceptance. No further details to add for this point unless a complication emerges immediately. All notes provided to hospital on arrival for complete integration into EMS medical team handover and electronic transmission of medical notes to the care nurse waiting for her upon arrival for further procedures. Patient's GCS remains consistent and within normal limits as if not suffering from neurological impairment despite back and lower extremity symptomatology. No signs of disorientation, confusion, or altered mental status on initial or repeated assessments during transport. No signs of pallor or diaphoresis reported at any point from visual or verbal patient described inputs. Skin remains warm and dry with strong peripheral pulses per EMT observations here. No pending diagnostic procedures requested or started during EMT care but are expected to be addressed at hospital once patient is safely delivered onto their systems for institutional adoption into emergency care routine here per policies of facility managing trauma cases. All procedures and interventions by EMT team followed protocol as per expected for general trauma patient standard of care and pre-hospital treatment intent for motion injuries. No signs of hemorrhage, shock, or need for immediate intubation on scene as per field findings. No emergency medications administered due to challenges identified at given stage. Ultimate care goals have been reassigned post-handover with hospital procedures in place for capping patient needs and stabilizing condition as needed post-admission for next available step in acute care. All documentation is finalized and EMT team can safely return to base for call coordination updates. No contraindications for continued care in facility exist upon completion and handover as noted before patient arrival to immediate care zone for facility-based stabilization and evaluations. Any neurological developments to be observed will be monitored as updates ensue post-admission here.
